T1055.simple: Imagine you have a calculator app running on your computer. Process injection is like sneaking code into that calculator so it secretly does evil things while looking completely normal. Security software watches for new programs starting up, so malware hides inside programs that are already trusted and running — your antivirus doesn't notice because it looks like the calculator is doing all the work.
T1055.real_world_example: Used in the Lazarus Group's WannaCry campaign (2017). The malware injected code into legitimate Windows services to spread across 230,000 computers in 150 countries, crippling hospitals, banks, and telecoms with billions in damage.

T1055.001.simple: This is one specific way of hiding inside another program. The malware tells Windows to load a special 'plugin file' (a DLL) into a running program it doesn't belong in. Once loaded, that plugin can do anything the program can do — access the internet, read files, steal passwords — all disguised as a normal program.
T1055.001.real_world_example: The Dridex banking trojan (2015-2020) used DLL injection to insert itself into browser processes, silently intercepting online banking sessions. It stole over $100 million from bank accounts across 40 countries.

T1055.012.simple: Process hollowing is like identity theft for programs. The malware starts a legitimate Windows program (like notepad.exe), immediately puts it on pause, hollows out its insides, stuffs its own evil code inside, then lets it run. Task Manager shows notepad.exe running, but it's actually the malware in disguise.
T1055.012.real_world_example: APT41, a Chinese state-sponsored group, used process hollowing in their 2019 campaign against healthcare and telecom companies. They hollowed out legitimate Windows utilities to hide backdoors that persisted for months undetected.

T1622.simple: A debugger is a special tool security researchers use to watch malware run in slow motion, step by step. This technique is the malware checking 'is someone watching me right now?' — like a criminal who acts normal when they notice a security camera. If it detects the researcher's tools, it does nothing suspicious. Once no one's watching, it does the real damage.
T1622.real_world_example: The Ramnit banking trojan used IsDebuggerPresent and timing checks to detect analyst tools. When it detected debugging, it would crash or behave normally, making analysis much harder for security researchers.

T1497.simple: Security companies often test suspicious programs in a fake computer environment called a 'sandbox' — like a glass box they can observe safely. This technique is the malware trying to figure out if it's in that fake box. It looks for clues (unusual hardware specs, too-clean file systems, timing oddities). If it thinks it's being watched in a sandbox, it plays completely innocent. On a real computer, it attacks.
T1497.real_world_example: The Carbanak gang used VM detection in their attacks on over 100 banks (2013-2018). Their malware checked for sandbox environments before stealing over $1 billion by manipulating bank transaction systems.

T1497.003.simple: Sandboxes used by security tools usually only let a program run for a short time before analyzing it. So some malware just sleeps — it waits and does nothing for a long time, hoping the sandbox gives up and marks it as safe. The malware then wakes up and attacks later when no one expects it.
T1497.003.real_world_example: Stuxnet (2010) used time-based checks to slow execution in sandbox environments. The worm checked system uptime and CPU load before activating its payload, helping it evade automated analysis systems used by Iranian nuclear facilities.

T1027.simple: Antivirus software recognizes malware by its code, like recognizing a face. This technique is malware wearing a disguise — it scrambles and encrypts its own code so security tools don't recognize it. The disguise only comes off when the malware actually needs to run. It's like a note written in a secret code that decodes itself when you hold it up to the light.
T1027.real_world_example: The Emotet malware (2018-2021) heavily obfuscated its code to evade detection, changing its appearance with every infection. At its peak, it was the world's most dangerous malware, infecting 1.6 million machines.

T1027.002.simple: A packer squishes and encrypts a program into a tiny bundle, like vacuum-sealing it. When the bundle runs, it unpacks itself in memory. Legitimate programs do this to save space, but malware does it to hide. Antivirus sees the packed bundle and doesn't recognize it as dangerous — the real malicious code only appears in memory at runtime, hidden from scanners.
T1027.002.real_world_example: Zeus banking trojan (2007–2010) used a custom UPX-based packer that changed signature with each build. This packing helped it infect over 3.6 million computers in the US alone, stealing banking credentials worth hundreds of millions.

T1562.simple: Your computer has security tools running — antivirus, firewall, Windows Defender, event logs. This technique is the malware turning those off, like a burglar disabling the alarm system before robbing a bank. Once the defenders are blind, the malware can do whatever it wants without getting caught or leaving traces.
T1562.real_world_example: The REvil ransomware group disabled Windows Defender's real-time protection and tamper protection before deploying encryption. Their Kaseya supply chain attack (2021) hit 1,500 businesses through a single software update.

T1140.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
T1140.simple: The program decodes or decrypts hidden data at runtime.
T1140.detailed: Uses CryptDecrypt, Base64 decoding functions, or XOR loops to decode obfuscated strings, configuration data, or payloads. The encoded content is invisible to static string extraction until decoded.
T1140.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike's Beacon stores its C2 configuration (server addresses, sleep times, watermark) in an XOR-encrypted blob that is decoded at runtime.

T1036.simple: This is malware in costume. It might name itself 'svchost.exe' (a real, trusted Windows process), use a fake Microsoft icon, or copy itself into a Windows system folder. The goal is to fool users into thinking it's a normal part of Windows. Like a thief wearing a security guard uniform to blend in.
T1036.real_world_example: APT10 (Stone Panda) renamed their tools to match legitimate Windows utilities during the Cloud Hopper campaign (2016–2019), targeting managed service providers in 45 countries to reach their actual targets through trusted supply chains.

T1070.simple: Windows keeps a record of everything that happens — programs run, files changed, logins attempted — in event logs. This is like your computer's diary. After an attack, forensic investigators read this diary to figure out what happened. This technique is the malware tearing out the relevant pages of that diary to destroy the evidence of what it did.
T1070.real_world_example: APT41 systematically cleared Windows Event Logs after each stage of their intrusions into telecom companies. Without logs, incident responders couldn't determine how long the attackers had been inside or what they accessed.

T1574.simple: When Windows programs need extra code, they load files called DLLs. Windows searches for them in a specific order of folders. This technique exploits that search order — the malware places its own fake DLL in a folder that Windows checks first. A legitimate program then accidentally loads the fake DLL instead of the real one, giving the malware a free ride.
T1574.real_world_example: Axiom Group used execution flow hijacking in their decade-long espionage campaign against aerospace, energy, and government targets across 25 countries, discovered and reported by Novetta in 2014.

T1547.simple: Malware wants to survive after you restart your computer. This technique adds the malware to your computer's 'startup list' — a set of registry entries or folders that Windows automatically runs every time you log in. It's like writing your name on the school attendance list for every future class, even ones you don't sign up for.
T1547.real_world_example: The NotPetya attack (2017) modified boot records and registry run keys to ensure persistence. Originally targeting Ukraine, it spread globally causing $10 billion in damage — the most destructive cyberattack in history.

T1547.001.simple: The Windows Registry has special keys that list programs to run every time a user logs in. This is one of the most common ways malware ensures it restarts after every reboot. It's like sticking a reminder note inside your front door so you always see it when you come home — except the note starts up malware instead of reminding you of groceries.
T1547.001.real_world_example: Agent Tesla, a popular info-stealer, adds itself to the Windows Registry Run key so it starts every time you log in. Sold as a 'legitimate monitoring tool', it has been used in thousands of business email compromise attacks.

T1053.simple: Windows has a built-in alarm clock for programs called Task Scheduler. Legitimate software uses it to run updates or backups. Malware abuses it to schedule itself to run regularly, or at a specific time when the computer is less monitored — like midnight. Even if you stop the malware manually, the scheduled task will restart it later.
T1053.real_world_example: Emotet (2014–2021) created scheduled tasks named after legitimate Windows processes to re-launch itself after reboot. This persistence mechanism survived reboots and manual process-killing, making it one of the hardest botnets to eradicate.

T1112.simple: The Windows Registry is like a giant settings book that controls almost everything about how Windows behaves. Malware writes to it to change rules — disabling security features, adding itself to startup, hiding files. It's like sneaking into the principal's office and rewriting the school rules so you never get in trouble.
T1112.real_world_example: Turla (Snake/Uroburos), a Russian FSB-linked group, stored encrypted backdoor code inside Windows Registry keys. This fileless persistence technique made their implants invisible to traditional file-based antivirus scanning.

T1134.simple: In Windows, every running program carries an 'identity badge' (access token) that says what it's allowed to do. Administrators have powerful badges. This technique is the malware stealing or copying an administrator's badge and using it for itself — suddenly able to do anything an admin can do, like install software, change settings, or access any file.
T1134.real_world_example: During the 2014 Sony Pictures hack, the Lazarus Group manipulated access tokens to elevate privileges on compromised Windows systems, enabling them to deploy the Destover wiper malware that destroyed 70% of Sony's IT infrastructure.

T1056.simple: Your keyboard is the main way you communicate with your computer — and everything you type (passwords, credit cards, messages) goes through it. Keylogging is secretly recording every single keystroke. The malware silently logs what you type and sends it to the attacker. They can reconstruct your passwords and private messages from the recording.
T1056.real_world_example: FinFisher (FinSpy) surveillance software, sold to governments worldwide and exposed by Citizen Lab (2012–2017), used input capture to harvest credentials from dissidents and journalists in 25+ countries.

T1056.001.simple: This is the classic spy technique applied to computers. The malware installs an invisible hook into Windows that intercepts every key you press before the key reaches any program. You type your bank password into your browser — the keylogger captures it before the browser even sees it. The recording is sent to the attacker.
T1056.001.real_world_example: The GameOver Zeus botnet (2011–2014) used a keylogger to steal online banking credentials from over 1 million computers. Before it was taken down in Operation Tovar (2014), it had stolen an estimated $100 million.

T1113.name: Screen Capture
T1113.simple: The program takes screenshots of the user's screen.
T1113.detailed: Uses BitBlt, GetDC, GetDesktopWindow, or PrintWindow to capture screen contents. Found in RATs, spyware, and surveillance software.
T1113.real_world_example: APT37 (Reaper), a North Korean group, captured screenshots every few seconds from South Korean aerospace and media company systems in 2017.

T1003.simple: Windows stores hashed copies of passwords in memory so it doesn't need to ask you to log in again constantly. This technique extracts those stored password hashes from Windows memory. The attacker can then use those hashes directly to log into other computers on the network — they don't even need to know the real password. It's like finding a duplicate key hidden under a doormat.
T1003.real_world_example: Mimikatz, the famous credential dumping tool, was used in the 2017 NotPetya attack to harvest passwords from memory and spread across networks. One Ukrainian accounting firm's compromised update infected companies in 65 countries.

T1082.name: System Information Discovery
T1082.simple: The program gathers details about the computer — OS version, hardware, hostname.
T1082.detailed: Calls GetSystemInfo, GetComputerName, GetVersionEx, or reads system environment variables. Used to fingerprint the victim for targeted payloads or to detect virtual machines.
T1082.real_world_example: Lazarus Group's AppleJeus cryptocurrency malware used GetSystemInfo to check processor count and memory — VMs typically have fewer cores, revealing a sandbox.

T1083.name: File and Directory Discovery
T1083.simple: The program searches for files on the system.
T1083.detailed: Uses FindFirstFile/FindNextFile to enumerate files. Ransomware uses this to find files to encrypt. Info-stealers search for browser data, SSH keys, and cryptocurrency wallets.
T1083.real_world_example: LockBit ransomware used FindFirstFile to recursively enumerate all files on local and mapped network drives before encrypting them, skipping only system-critical directories.

T1057.simple: This technique lists all programs currently running on the computer. Malware does this to look for security tools (antivirus, debuggers) to avoid, find valuable targets (browsers, email clients that store credentials), or check that its own processes are running. It's like a spy cataloguing who's in the building.
T1057.real_world_example: TrickBot operators enumerated running processes to identify security tools and corporate environments before dropping ransomware. This reconnaissance determined whether targets were business networks worth encrypting for maximum ransom.

T1106.name: Native API
T1106.simple: The program calls low-level Windows NT functions directly, bypassing normal API layers.
T1106.detailed: Imports from ntdll.dll (NtCreateThread, NtMapViewOfSection, NtAllocateVirtualMemory) bypass user-mode API hooks installed by security products. This is a strong evasion signal.
T1106.real_world_example: Gh0st RAT used direct ntdll calls in the GhostNet espionage campaign against the Dalai Lama's offices across 103 countries, evading security tools that only hooked kernel32.

T1059.simple: PowerShell and cmd.exe are legitimate, built-in Windows tools for running commands. Because they're built into Windows, they're trusted by default. Malware abuses them to run commands that do the real damage — download files, change settings, delete backups — because using a trusted built-in tool looks less suspicious than running a custom malware executable.
T1059.real_world_example: The SolarWinds attack (2020) used PowerShell commands extensively. APT29 (Cozy Bear) used encoded PowerShell scripts to move laterally through 18,000 organisations including US government agencies.

T1021.simple: Once inside one computer, attackers want to spread to other computers on the same network. Remote desktop and other remote services are designed to let you control a computer from far away. Attackers use stolen credentials to log into other computers on the network as if they're sitting right in front of them, spreading their access across the organization.
T1021.real_world_example: APT33 (Elfin), an Iranian state group, used legitimate remote services including VPN and RDP to move laterally inside networks of Saudi Arabian petrochemical companies and US aerospace firms during their 2017–2018 campaigns.

T1071.simple: Malware needs to communicate with its controller (the attacker) to receive instructions and send back stolen data. Firewalls block weird traffic, but they allow normal web browsing. So malware hides its spy communications inside regular-looking HTTP web traffic or DNS queries, blending in with everyone else's internet use. It's like writing secret messages in what looks like ordinary letters.
T1071.real_world_example: APT29's SolarWinds campaign (2020) disguised its command-and-control traffic as normal Orion software updates. The legitimate-looking HTTP traffic allowed them to remain undetected inside government networks for 9 months.

T1105.simple: The first piece of malware that lands on a computer is often just a small 'downloader'. Once it's inside, it reaches out to the attacker's server and downloads the real tools — password stealers, ransomware, remote access software. It's like a small scout breaking into a building and then opening a window so the rest of the team can climb in with all the equipment.
T1105.real_world_example: During the DNC breach (2016), APT28 (Fancy Bear) downloaded additional tools including Mimikatz and X-Agent directly to compromised systems. The FBI and CISA later attributed this campaign to Russian GRU Unit 26165.

T1041.simple: After stealing data, malware needs to get it back to the attacker without being detected. This technique sends the stolen information through the same channel the malware already uses to receive commands. Since that channel already blends in as normal traffic, the stolen data slips out unnoticed — hidden inside what looks like regular communication.
T1041.real_world_example: APT1 (Comment Crew), exposed in Mandiant's landmark 2013 report, exfiltrated hundreds of terabytes of intellectual property from 141 US companies over 7 years — all via encrypted channels blending with normal HTTPS traffic.

T1486.simple: Ransomware scrambles all your files — photos, documents, videos — with encryption so you can't open them anymore. It's like someone changing the lock on your filing cabinet and taking the only key. The attacker then demands money to give you the key. Without it, your files are just scrambled nonsense, effectively destroyed unless you pay up or restore from backup.
T1486.real_world_example: The Colonial Pipeline attack (2021) used DarkSide ransomware to encrypt critical fuel infrastructure systems. The attack caused fuel shortages across the US East Coast, and Colonial paid a $4.4 million ransom.

T1489.name: Service Stop
T1489.detailed: Uses ControlService or OpenService to stop services. Ransomware stops database services, backup agents, and security software before encrypting files.
T1489.real_world_example: Ryuk ransomware stopped 40+ services including SQL Server, Exchange, and backup agents before encryption, ensuring maximum damage and preventing recovery.

T1490.simple: Windows has safety nets: System Restore points, Volume Shadow Copies (automatic backups of your files), and recovery partitions. These are what help you recover from disasters. Ransomware destroys these safety nets before encrypting your files — deleting the backups — so you can't simply restore to before the attack. The ransom becomes your only option.
T1490.real_world_example: Ryuk ransomware operators routinely deleted Windows Volume Shadow Copies using vssadmin.exe before encrypting, making recovery without paying the ransom nearly impossible. They used this technique in the 2020 Universal Health Services attack affecting 400 US hospitals.

T1573.simple: When malware communicates with its attacker, security tools monitor that traffic looking for suspicious patterns. Encrypted channels scramble the communications so monitoring tools can't read what's being said. It's like whispering into a phone while in a crowded room — people might see you talking, but can't hear what you're actually saying.
T1573.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike, used by dozens of APT groups and criminal operators including those behind the 2021 Microsoft Exchange attacks (Hafnium), uses custom encrypted channels for C2 communication that blend with legitimate HTTPS traffic to avoid network detection.

T1018.simple: When attackers want to spread through a network, they first need a map. This technique scans the network for other computers — what IP addresses exist, what hostnames are used, what services are running. It's like the malware drawing a map of the neighborhood before deciding which houses to break into next.
T1018.real_world_example: WannaCry (2017) scanned entire IP ranges looking for systems vulnerable to EternalBlue (MS17-010). This aggressive network discovery enabled it to infect 230,000 systems across 150 countries within 24 hours of initial release.

T1068.simple: Software has bugs. Sometimes those bugs let a program do things it shouldn't — like gaining administrator powers from a limited account. This technique exploits one of those bugs to 'jump ranks', going from a limited user account to full administrator control. It's like finding a loophole in school rules that technically lets a student override a teacher's decision.
T1068.real_world_example: EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144), developed by the NSA and leaked by Shadow Brokers in 2017, exploited a Windows SMB vulnerability for privilege escalation. It was weaponised in both WannaCry and NotPetya, causing a combined $20+ billion in damage.

T1548.002.simple: Windows UAC (User Account Control) is the popup that asks 'Do you want to allow this program to make changes to your device?' Bypassing UAC means the malware gains administrator privileges without that popup ever appearing. You never get asked for permission — the malware just silently promotes itself to full admin.
T1548.002.real_world_example: The Bypass UAC technique was used by the Carbanak group in their attacks on over 100 banks globally (2013–2015). By bypassing UAC silently, they could install persistent backdoors without triggering the user consent dialogs that might alert a victim.

T1548.simple: Modern operating systems have controls designed to prevent programs from quietly gaining too many privileges. This is the general category for all the tricks attackers use to silently promote themselves from a regular user to an administrator without any permission popup appearing. Think of it as finding an unofficial side entrance into a secure area that bypasses the main security desk entirely.
T1548.real_world_example: Lazarus Group used elevation control abuse in the $81 million Bangladesh Bank heist (2016), the largest bank theft in history. They escalated privileges on SWIFT terminal systems to send fraudulent transfer requests before Bangladesh Bank staff woke up.

T1543.simple: Windows has many background programs called services that run automatically every time the computer starts — like the printer service, Windows Update, and antivirus. This technique installs malware as a fake official service using standard Windows commands. Once registered, it runs automatically with every reboot, often with high system privileges, blending in with dozens of legitimate services. Most users never look at the service list, so this can persist undetected for months — like a criminal who gets a job as the building's janitor so they have an official reason to be there every single morning.
T1543.real_world_example: BlackEnergy malware, used in the December 2015 Ukrainian power grid attack attributed to Russian Sandworm, installed itself as a system process to survive reboots. The attack left 230,000 Ukrainians without power for up to 6 hours.

T1543.003.simple: Every time Windows starts, it automatically launches a list of approved background programs called services. Attackers install their malware as one of those approved services using the official Windows service manager commands. Once installed as a service, the malware runs automatically with every reboot with high system privileges, hidden among dozens of real services with names like 'Windows Update Helper' or 'Network Service Manager'. It's like adding yourself to the company payroll — officially authorized to show up every day.
T1543.003.real_world_example: Stuxnet (2010) installed two malicious Windows services (mrxcls.sys and mrxnet.sys) disguised as legitimate rootkit drivers. These services loaded automatically at boot, giving the worm persistent access to Siemens SCADA systems controlling uranium enrichment centrifuges.

T1115.simple: Your clipboard is the invisible holding area for anything you copy with Ctrl+C — passwords, credit card numbers, crypto wallet addresses, personal messages. This technique secretly reads whatever is in your clipboard without you knowing. Every time you copy a password from your password manager or copy a Bitcoin address to paste into an exchange, the malware intercepts it. It's like someone reading over your shoulder every time you copy text, keeping their own secret copy of everything you've copied.
T1115.real_world_example: ComboJack (2018) and its successor Evrial silently monitored the clipboard for cryptocurrency wallet addresses, replacing them with attacker-controlled wallets. Researchers estimated ComboJack infected hundreds of thousands of machines, redirecting an unknown volume of transactions.

T1055.002.simple: Instead of sneaking in through a DLL plugin file, this technique directly copies raw malicious code into another program's memory and forces it to execute. Imagine taking your own written instructions, folding them up, and physically inserting them into someone else's notebook — then tricking the teacher into reading from their notebook. The malicious code runs inside a trusted program's memory, making it look like that normal program did everything.
T1055.002.real_world_example: REvil (Sodinokibi) ransomware used PE injection to run its payload inside legitimate Windows processes. REvil earned over $100 million before operators were arrested in 2022 following the Kaseya attack that affected 1,500+ businesses.

T1055.011.simple: Windows programs that create visible windows (like your browser or Notepad) have a small reserved memory area attached to each window. This technique hides malicious code inside that spare memory space and then tricks a legitimate program into reading and executing it. It's like hiding a secret message inside the empty back cover of a library book — the librarian keeps it on the shelf, loans it out, and the next reader unknowingly passes on the hidden message.
T1055.011.real_world_example: Duqu 2.0 (2014–2015), attributed to the Israeli Unit 8200, used Extra Window Memory injection to persist entirely in memory during espionage operations against Kaspersky Lab and European P5+1 Iran nuclear deal negotiations.

T1497.001.simple: Security sandboxes (fake analysis computers) often run on unusual virtual hardware with giveaway characteristics — unusual screen resolution, too much or too little RAM, a brand new hard drive with no real history, or a CPU that reports itself as virtual. This technique makes the malware do a hardware and system inspection before doing anything suspicious. If the results look like a test machine rather than a real person's computer, the malware stays completely silent. On a real computer, it attacks.
T1497.001.real_world_example: TrickBot (2016-2022) checked for VirtualBox, VMware, and Hyper-V before executing. This helped it avoid analysis labs while infecting millions of real computers, often delivering ransomware as a second stage.

T1027.007.simple: Antivirus software can scan a program and spot dangerous function calls like 'open this network connection' or 'steal this password'. This technique hides those dangerous calls from the scanner by not writing them directly in the code. Instead, the malware calculates where the functions are in memory at runtime — like a spy who memorizes their mission rather than writing it down. When the scanner looks at the program, it finds nothing suspicious. The real actions only happen when it's actually running.
T1027.007.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike's Beacon implant, used in hundreds of APT campaigns, resolves Windows API calls at runtime using hashed function names. This technique was a key evasion mechanism in the 2020 SolarWinds supply chain attack affecting 18,000 organisations.

T1562.001.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
T1562.001.detailed: Terminates AV processes, stops security services, or modifies their configuration. Detected via TerminateProcess imports combined with strings referencing security product names.
T1562.001.real_world_example: BlackCat (ALPHV) ransomware killed 160+ security-related processes and services before encrypting, including Sophos, Symantec, McAfee, and Windows Defender.

T1562.002.simple: Windows automatically keeps a detailed record of everything that happens — logins, programs launched, files changed, errors — called Event Logs. Security software and forensic investigators read these logs constantly to catch malware and investigate incidents. This technique turns off Windows event logging entirely or clears the existing logs, making it nearly impossible to figure out what happened during an attack. It's like shredding every security camera recording and visitor log from the day of a bank robbery.
T1562.002.real_world_example: APT41 disabled Windows Event Logging across dozens of compromised servers during their 2020 espionage campaign targeting healthcare and government networks. By silencing the logging service, credential theft and lateral movement left no trace in Event Viewer, delaying discovery by months.

T1036.001.simple: Windows programs can have a digital signature — a verified seal that proves a trusted company like Microsoft or Adobe created the file. Security tools often trust signed programs and flag unsigned ones. This technique fakes a signature or copies an existing one to make malware appear signed and trustworthy. It's like laminating a photocopy of an ID card — a quick glance might fool someone who isn't looking closely, but the real verification system would catch it.
T1036.001.real_world_example: Flame (Flamer/sKyWIper, 2012) used a forged Microsoft certificate generated via an MD5 collision attack to sign malicious modules. This allowed it to pass Windows Update verification and infect air-gapped networks across the Middle East.

T1070.001.simple: Windows Event Logs are like your computer's diary — they record every login attempt, every program that ran, every file that changed, and every error. Security teams and forensic investigators read this diary after an attack to figure out exactly what happened and when. This technique deletes all those diary entries after a breach to erase the footprints. It's like a thief who not only robbed the museum but also erased every security camera recording from that night before walking out the door.
T1070.001.real_world_example: Hafnium, a Chinese state-sponsored group, cleared Windows Event Logs after exploiting ProxyLogon vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange servers in early 2021. The attack compromised an estimated 250,000+ servers globally before patches were available.

T1574.001.simple: When a program needs to load extra code, Windows searches a series of folders in a specific order looking for the right file. This technique places a malicious fake file with the right name in a folder that Windows checks before the real one. A legitimate, trusted program then accidentally loads and runs the fake file. It's like swapping a real prescription medicine bottle with a fake one that looks identical — the patient takes what they think is the real medication, not knowing it's been replaced.
T1574.001.real_world_example: The 2020 SolarWinds supply chain attack included DLL search order hijacking as part of SUNBURST's loading mechanism. Attackers placed a malicious Orion update DLL that was found before the legitimate one, affecting 18,000 customers including US Treasury and Commerce departments.

T1574.002.simple: Some software comes packaged with its own helper files stored right next to the main program. When that program runs, it automatically loads nearby files it expects to be there. This technique places a malicious fake helper file next to a legitimate program. When the real program launches, it picks up the attacker's fake file and runs it, unknowingly giving the malware all the trusted program's permissions. It's like a con artist who walks into a building right next to a real employee — security assumes they belong together.
T1574.002.real_world_example: Higaisa APT (a suspected North Korean-linked group) used DLL side-loading via legitimate Slack, Zoom, and TeamViewer executables in their 2020 campaign against Chinese government officials, exploiting trust in recognisable applications.

T1053.005.simple: Windows Task Scheduler is a built-in alarm clock for programs — it can automatically run any program at a specific time, on a specific day, or every time someone logs in. Malware creates a scheduled task to restart itself regularly, even if you manually kill it. You could notice the malware and stop it, but an hour later Task Scheduler silently starts it up again. It's like setting 50 recurring alarms on someone's phone that they don't know about — they can't stop the alarm by just turning it off once.
T1053.005.real_world_example: Hafnium (ProxyLogon, 2021) created Windows Scheduled Tasks to maintain persistence on compromised Exchange servers. Microsoft reported that attackers used scheduled tasks to run web shells even after initial access points were patched.

T1134.001.simple: Every program running in Windows carries an invisible ID badge (access token) that determines what it's allowed to do. Administrator accounts have all-access badges. This technique steals a copy of an admin's badge and uses it to impersonate them — suddenly the malware can install software, change security settings, and access any file, all while Windows thinks a real administrator is making those requests. It's like finding a keycard that opens every door in a building and quietly making a copy of it.
T1134.001.real_world_example: The Uber breach (2022) involved token impersonation after an attacker used Mimikatz-derived techniques to steal and reuse administrator tokens, ultimately accessing 57 million customer and driver records stored in AWS.

T1134.002.simple: After stealing an administrator's access token (their identity badge), this technique uses it to launch a brand new program that is born already authorized as that administrator. The new process inherits all the stolen privileges from birth. It's like using a stolen master key to not only enter a restricted area yourself, but to invite your entire team through the same door, each of them walking in with full authorized access.
T1134.002.real_world_example: APT29 (Cozy Bear) used CreateProcessWithToken during their SolarWinds lateral movement to create new processes under SYSTEM context, enabling them to access Microsoft 365 tenants and Active Directory environments of government agencies.

T1059.001.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
T1059.001.simple: The program uses PowerShell to run commands.
T1059.001.detailed: PowerShell strings in a binary (powershell.exe, -EncodedCommand, Invoke-Expression, IEX) indicate the program spawns PowerShell for execution. This is the most common fileless malware technique.
T1059.001.real_world_example: Emotet used PowerShell download cradles (IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString()) to fetch and execute its loader, avoiding dropping files to disk.

T1059.003.simple: cmd.exe is the original Windows command prompt — the black text window for running commands. Because it's a core part of Windows itself, it's trusted and almost never blocked. Malware uses it to chain together commands that download files, change settings, create accounts, or delete evidence. Even if the malware itself would be caught, using the built-in command prompt makes the activity look like normal system administration. It's like committing a crime using the city's own official tools and vehicles.
T1059.003.real_world_example: FIN7 used Windows Command Shell extensively in their spear-phishing campaigns against US restaurant chains (2015–2018). Their LNK files dropped cmd.exe scripts that stole 15 million payment cards from Chipotle, Arby's, and other chains.

T1021.001.simple: Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) lets you control a Windows computer from anywhere in the world as if you were sitting right in front of it — you see the screen, move the mouse, and type on the keyboard. Attackers who steal a user's login password can use RDP to take full interactive control of that computer and every other computer on the network they can reach. It's like using a stolen front door key to walk right in, sit at the victim's desk, and have complete access to everything they could access.
T1021.001.real_world_example: Conti ransomware operators used compromised RDP credentials to laterally move through hospital networks during the COVID-19 pandemic. Their 2020 attack on Universal Health Services, one of the largest US hospital chains, forced staff to use pen and paper for patient care for weeks.

T1071.001.simple: Firewalls block unusual network traffic but almost never block regular web browsing because that would break the internet for everyone. This technique hides the malware's spy communications — receiving commands from attackers, sending back stolen data — inside normal-looking HTTP or HTTPS web traffic. Firewalls see what looks like someone visiting a website, when really it's the malware calling home to its controller. It's like writing secret orders in invisible ink on what looks like an ordinary letter.
T1071.001.real_world_example: The OceanLotus group (APT32) from Vietnam used HTTPS callbacks disguised as legitimate cloud service traffic to control compromised machines in Southeast Asian companies. The traffic blended perfectly with normal web browsing.

T1071.004.simple: Every time your computer visits a website, it first does a DNS lookup — like checking a phone book to find the website's real address. Almost every network allows DNS traffic through without inspecting it carefully. This technique hides malware commands and stolen data encoded inside DNS lookup messages. The malware can receive instructions and send back stolen information without making any normal-looking network connection. It's like passing secret messages hidden inside the address written on the envelope — nobody checks the address, they just deliver the letter.
T1071.004.real_world_example: SUNBURST (SolarWinds, 2020) used DNS for covert C2 communication, encoding commands in DNS subdomain queries to avoid IDS/IPS detection. The DNS traffic appeared as normal Orion telemetry, helping APT29 remain undetected for 9 months.

T1003.001.simple: Windows keeps a copy of your login credentials in a special system process called LSASS — the part of Windows that handles who's logged in. This process holds password hashes and sometimes plaintext passwords in memory. This technique extracts that credential data directly from LSASS memory using special tools. With those stolen credentials, attackers can log into any other computer on the network as the real user, without ever needing to guess or crack the actual password. It's like finding the master password notebook kept in the security office.
T1003.001.real_world_example: APT29 used LSASS memory dumping during the SolarWinds breach to harvest administrator credentials, allowing them to move freely across Microsoft 365 and Azure environments of multiple US government agencies.

T1005.simple: Before stealing information, malware needs to find what's worth taking. This technique searches the local computer's file system for valuable files — documents with passwords, configuration files containing database credentials, spreadsheets with financial data, or anything else the attacker wants. It's like a burglar who methodically goes through every drawer and cabinet before deciding what to put in their bag. The search happens silently in the background while you're working, and you'd never know.
T1005.real_world_example: The Lazarus Group systematically searched local file systems for documents, source code, and cryptocurrency wallet files during their 2022 attacks on blockchain firms. The FBI attributed $1.7 billion in cryptocurrency theft that year to North Korean actors using this collection technique.

T1518.name: Software Discovery
T1518.simple: The program checks what software is installed.
T1518.detailed: Reads the Uninstall registry key, queries WMI, or enumerates installed programs. Used to find security software to disable or to identify target applications.
T1518.real_world_example: TrickBot checked for installed security products (antivirus, EDR) and sent the list to its C2, which then provided specific evasion instructions for each vendor.

T1518.001.simple: This technique specifically looks for security software — antivirus, endpoint detection, firewalls — running on the target system. Knowing what defenses are in place lets the attacker choose the right evasion strategy. It's like a spy identifying all the security cameras before planning their route through a building.
T1518.001.real_world_example: The LockBit ransomware group actively detected and disabled Windows Defender, Sophos, and other security tools before encrypting files. They became the most active ransomware group in 2022, attacking over 1,000 organisations.

T1007.name: System Service Discovery
T1007.simple: The program checks what services are running on the system.
T1007.detailed: Queries the Service Control Manager to list installed services, their status, and configuration. Malware uses this to find security software to disable or to identify the system's role.
T1007.real_world_example: TrickBot enumerated Windows services to identify and disable security products before deploying Ryuk ransomware in hospital networks during the COVID-19 pandemic.

T1010.name: Application Window Discovery
T1010.simple: The program checks what windows are open on screen.
T1010.detailed: Uses EnumWindows or FindWindow to list visible application windows. Malware uses this to detect analysis tools (OllyDbg, IDA Pro, Wireshark) and alter behavior when observed.
T1010.real_world_example: The Ramnit banking trojan checked for open browser windows to identify when the user was visiting banking sites, then injected its credential-stealing form overlays.

T1012.name: Query Registry
T1012.simple: The program reads Windows Registry values to learn about the system.
T1012.detailed: Reads registry keys to discover installed software, OS configuration, security settings, or persistence mechanisms left by other malware. Common queries target Run keys, Uninstall keys, and security product registration.
T1012.real_world_example: APT29 (Cozy Bear) queried the registry for installed security products during the SolarWinds campaign, adapting their behavior to avoid detection by specific vendors.

T1016.name: System Network Configuration Discovery
T1016.simple: The program checks network settings like IP addresses and DNS servers.
T1016.detailed: Uses GetAdaptersInfo, GetAdaptersAddresses, or reads network configuration from the registry. Malware profiles the victim's network to plan lateral movement.
T1016.real_world_example: WannaCry (2017) enumerated network interfaces and subnet masks to identify targets for its SMB exploit, enabling it to spread to 230,000 systems across 150 countries.

T1033.name: System Owner/User Discovery
T1033.simple: The program checks who is logged in.
T1033.detailed: Calls GetUserName, LookupAccountSid, or reads environment variables to identify the current user. Used for victim profiling and targeting high-value accounts.
T1033.real_world_example: FIN7 used GetUserName to identify whether compromised systems belonged to finance department users before deploying their card-skimming malware.

T1047.name: Windows Management Instrumentation
T1047.simple: The program uses WMI to run commands or gather system information.
T1047.detailed: WMI provides a powerful interface for system management. Malware abuses it for execution (spawning processes), persistence (event subscriptions), and discovery (querying hardware/software).
T1047.real_world_example: APT29 used WMI event subscriptions for persistence during the SolarWinds supply chain attack, creating subscriptions that survived reboots and triggered on system events.

T1055.003.name: Process Injection: Thread Execution Hijacking
T1055.003.simple: The program hijacks an existing thread in another process to run its code.
T1055.003.detailed: Suspends a thread, overwrites its context to point to injected code, then resumes it. Unlike CreateRemoteThread, this reuses an existing thread, making detection harder.
T1055.003.real_world_example: The Carbanak banking group used thread hijacking to inject their backdoor into legitimate banking software processes, evading endpoint detection.

T1055.004.name: Process Injection: Asynchronous Procedure Call
T1055.004.simple: The program queues code to run inside another process using APC injection.
T1055.004.detailed: APC (Asynchronous Procedure Call) injection queues a function to run when a target thread enters an alertable wait state. Detected via QueueUserAPC or NtQueueApcThread imports.
T1055.004.real_world_example: The FinFisher surveillance tool used APC injection to inject its monitoring code into browser processes, capturing credentials and browsing activity.

T1059.005.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Visual Basic
T1059.005.simple: The program uses VBScript or VBA for execution.
T1059.005.detailed: References to WScript.Shell, CreateObject, or VBA-related strings indicate script-based execution. Often found in Office macro malware or dropper scripts.
T1059.005.real_world_example: Dridex banking trojan was delivered through macro-enabled Word documents where VBA macros downloaded the main payload using WScript.Shell.

T1069.name: Permission Groups Discovery
T1069.simple: The program checks what user groups exist on the system.
T1069.detailed: Uses NetGroupEnum, NetLocalGroupEnum, or LDAP queries to discover local and domain groups. Identifies admin groups for privilege escalation targets.
T1069.real_world_example: APT28 (Fancy Bear) enumerated Active Directory groups during the DNC breach to identify domain admin accounts for lateral movement.

T1087.name: Account Discovery
T1087.simple: The program lists user accounts on the system or domain.
T1087.detailed: Uses NetUserEnum, LookupAccountSid, or LDAP queries to enumerate local and domain accounts. Identifies targets for credential theft and lateral movement.
T1087.real_world_example: APT1 (Comment Crew) enumerated domain accounts in 141 US companies over 7 years, identifying high-value targets for their intellectual property theft campaign.

T1119.name: Automated Collection
T1119.simple: The program automatically gathers files matching certain patterns.
T1119.detailed: Combines file discovery (FindFirstFile) with file reading to automatically collect documents, credentials, or other valuable data without user interaction.
T1119.real_world_example: APT1 used automated collection scripts to harvest all .doc, .pdf, and .ppt files from targeted systems, exfiltrating terabytes of intellectual property.

T1124.name: System Time Discovery
T1124.simple: The program checks the system clock.
T1124.detailed: Uses GetSystemTime, GetLocalTime, or NtQuerySystemTime. Malware checks time to detect sandboxes (which may have wrong dates), coordinate with C2 schedules, or implement time-based activation.
T1124.real_world_example: Stuxnet checked the system date to determine whether to activate its payload, only executing its centrifuge-sabotaging code during specific time windows.

T1129.name: Shared Modules
T1129.simple: The program loads DLLs at runtime using LoadLibrary.
T1129.detailed: Dynamic library loading with LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress allows a program to resolve and call functions at runtime rather than declaring them in the import table, hiding its true capabilities from static analysis.
T1129.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike's Beacon uses LoadLibrary and GetProcAddress to dynamically resolve all Windows API calls, making its import table appear completely benign.

T1135.name: Network Share Discovery
T1135.simple: The program searches for shared folders on the network.
T1135.detailed: Uses NetShareEnum or WNetEnumResource to find accessible network shares. Ransomware uses this to encrypt files on network drives, and worms use it to spread.
T1135.real_world_example: NotPetya (2017) enumerated network shares using NetShareEnum before encrypting them, causing $10 billion in damage and shutting down Maersk, Merck, and FedEx operations.

T1197.name: BITS Jobs
T1197.simple: The program uses the Background Intelligent Transfer Service to download files.
T1197.detailed: BITS is a legitimate Windows service for background downloads. Malware abuses it because BITS transfers are trusted by firewalls, survive reboots, and run under the SYSTEM context.
T1197.real_world_example: APT41 (Winnti) used BITS jobs to download second-stage payloads in their 2019-2020 campaign against video game companies, evading network monitoring.

T1218.005.name: System Binary Proxy Execution: Mshta
T1218.005.simple: The program uses mshta.exe to execute scripts.
T1218.005.detailed: Mshta.exe is a legitimate Windows binary that runs HTML Applications (.hta files). Malware uses it as a proxy to execute VBScript or JScript without directly launching wscript.exe.
T1218.005.real_world_example: APT32 (OceanLotus) used mshta.exe to execute their backdoor's initial stage, leveraging the trusted Microsoft binary to bypass application whitelisting.

T1218.011.name: System Binary Proxy Execution: Rundll32
T1218.011.simple: The program uses rundll32.exe to execute a DLL.
T1218.011.detailed: Rundll32 is a legitimate Windows utility that loads and runs DLL functions. Malware uses it to execute malicious DLLs under the guise of a trusted process.
T1218.011.real_world_example: Qbot banking trojan used rundll32.exe to load its main DLL payload, appearing as a legitimate system process in the task manager.

T1221.name: Template Injection
T1221.simple: The program fetches a malicious template from a remote server when opened.
T1221.detailed: Office documents can reference external templates via URLs in their .rels files. When opened, Office fetches the template — which can contain macros — bypassing email filters that only scan the original document.
T1221.real_world_example: Gamaredon (a Russian APT) used remote template injection in Word documents targeting Ukrainian government officials, fetching macro-enabled templates from C2 servers.

T1480.name: Execution Guardrails
T1480.simple: The program only runs its payload under specific conditions.
T1480.detailed: Checks for specific computer names, domains, IP ranges, or dates before executing. Prevents analysis in non-target environments and limits exposure.
T1480.real_world_example: Stuxnet only activated its centrifuge-sabotaging payload when it detected Siemens Step 7 SCADA software connected to specific Siemens S7-315/S7-417 PLCs.

T1485.name: Data Destruction
T1485.simple: The program destroys data on the system.
T1485.detailed: Overwrites files, wipes MBR/GPT, or corrupts file systems. Detected via imports for low-level disk access, file overwrite patterns, or MBR-writing code.
T1485.real_world_example: Shamoon (2012) overwrote the MBR of 35,000 Saudi Aramco computers with a burning American flag image, destroying data and rendering systems unbootable.

T1496.name: Resource Hijacking
T1496.simple: The program uses the computer's resources for the attacker's benefit.
T1496.detailed: Cryptomining malware that uses CPU/GPU for cryptocurrency mining. Detected via mining pool strings, mining algorithm constants, or references to popular mining software.
T1496.real_world_example: XMRig, the most common cryptominer in the wild, has been found embedded in thousands of trojanised applications, using victims' CPUs to mine Monero.

T1529.name: System Shutdown/Reboot
T1529.simple: The program shuts down or reboots the computer.
T1529.detailed: Uses ExitWindowsEx, InitiateSystemShutdown, or NtShutdownSystem. Ransomware reboots to activate modified boot sectors or to display ransom notes.
T1529.real_world_example: NotPetya forced a reboot after installing its modified MBR, displaying a fake CHKDSK screen while actually encrypting the file system.

T1546.001.name: Event Triggered Execution: Change Default File Association
T1546.001.simple: The program changes which program opens a file type.
T1546.001.detailed: Modifies registry keys under HKCR to change file associations (e.g., .txt opens malware instead of Notepad). Ensures execution whenever the user opens that file type.
T1546.001.real_world_example: The Astaroth trojan changed the .lnk file association to execute its payload whenever the user clicked any shortcut on their desktop.

T1547.009.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Shortcut Modification
T1547.009.simple: The program modifies desktop shortcuts to run malicious code.
T1547.009.detailed: Alters .lnk files in the Start Menu or Desktop to execute the malware alongside or instead of the legitimate program.
T1547.009.real_world_example: Higaisa APT modified Zoom and Slack shortcuts on victims' desktops to side-load malicious DLLs every time the user launched those applications.

T1553.002.name: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing
T1553.002.simple: The program uses stolen or forged code signing certificates.
T1553.002.detailed: Code signing makes software appear trusted. Malware signed with stolen certificates bypasses security checks. Detected by examining Authenticode signatures for revoked or suspicious certificates.
T1553.002.real_world_example: Flame malware (2012) used a forged Microsoft certificate created via an MD5 collision to sign its modules, passing Windows Update verification.

T1560.name: Archive Collected Data
T1560.simple: The program compresses or archives data before stealing it.
T1560.detailed: Uses ZIP, RAR, or custom compression to package stolen data. Compression reduces exfiltration size and can bypass DLP that doesn't inspect archives.
T1560.real_world_example: APT10 (Cloud Hopper) compressed stolen data into password-protected RAR archives before exfiltrating terabytes from managed service providers.

T1564.001.name: Hide Artifacts: Hidden Files and Directories
T1564.001.simple: The program hides files by setting the hidden attribute.
T1564.001.detailed: Uses SetFileAttributes to mark files as hidden and system, making them invisible in normal Explorer views. A basic but effective persistence technique.
T1564.001.real_world_example: Emotet set the hidden and system attributes on its dropped DLLs, hiding them from casual inspection in the user's AppData folder.

T1570.name: Lateral Tool Transfer
T1570.simple: The program copies tools to other computers on the network.
T1570.detailed: Uses CopyFile to network shares or WMI/PsExec to transfer payloads to other systems. Detected via network share APIs combined with file copy functions.
T1570.real_world_example: Conti ransomware operators used SMB file copies to distribute their encryptor to every system on the network before triggering simultaneous encryption.

T1614.name: System Location Discovery
T1614.simple: The program checks the computer's geographic location or language settings.
T1614.detailed: Reads GetLocaleInfo, GetUserDefaultUILanguage, or keyboard layout to determine the victim's country. Some malware avoids CIS countries to protect the operators.
T1614.real_world_example: GandCrab ransomware checked the keyboard layout and system locale, refusing to encrypt systems with Russian, Ukrainian, or other CIS language settings.

T1620.name: Reflective Code Loading
T1620.simple: The program loads code directly into memory without writing it to disk.
T1620.detailed: Manually maps a PE or shellcode into memory without using LoadLibrary, avoiding file-system-based detection. Detected via VirtualAlloc + manual PE header parsing patterns.
T1620.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike's Beacon uses reflective DLL injection to load its modules entirely in memory, leaving no DLL files on disk for forensic tools to find.

T1592.002.name: Gather Victim Host Information: Software
T1592.002.simple: Gather Victim Host Information: Software — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1592.002.detailed: Embedded strings referencing OS version queries, software inventory commands (`wmic`, `systeminfo`)
T1592.002.real_world_example: APT28 (Fancy Bear) gathered information about target software configurations during their 2016 DNC campaign.

T1589.001.name: Gather Victim Identity Information: Credentials
T1589.001.simple: Gather Victim Identity Information: Credentials — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1589.001.detailed: Embedded credential-harvesting strings (e.g., hardcoded credential paths like `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `NTDS.dit`)
T1589.001.real_world_example: Lazarus Group harvested employee email addresses from LinkedIn to craft spearphishing campaigns against Sony Pictures (2014).

T1587.001.name: Develop Capabilities: Malware
T1587.001.simple: Develop Capabilities: Malware — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1587.001.detailed: Packer signatures (UPX, Themida, VMProtect, ASPack), high entropy sections, anomalous PE structure, compiler artifacts from known malware toolkits
T1587.001.real_world_example: APT41 developed custom malware families including ShadowPad used in their 2019-2020 campaigns against telecommunications and gaming companies.

T1588.002.name: Obtain Capabilities: Tool
T1588.002.simple: Obtain Capabilities: Tool — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1588.002.detailed: Embedded tool signatures (e.g., Mimikatz strings, Cobalt Strike beacon config patterns, Metasploit shellcode byte patterns)
T1588.002.real_world_example: APT29 obtained and weaponized Cobalt Strike during the 2020 SolarWinds supply chain compromise.

T1608.001.name: Stage Capabilities: Upload Malware
T1608.001.simple: Stage Capabilities: Upload Malware — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1608.001.detailed: Embedded URLs/domains in strings referencing staging infrastructure
T1608.001.real_world_example: OceanLotus (APT32) staged malware on compromised legitimate websites during 2017-2019 campaigns targeting Southeast Asian governments.

T1059.006.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Python
T1059.006.simple: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Python — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1059.006.detailed: Embedded strings: `python.exe`, `python3`, `import os`, `import subprocess`; Python bytecode magic bytes; PyInstaller archive markers (`MEI`, `PYZ`)
T1059.006.real_world_example: Kimsuky used Python-based malware including BabyShark in 2019 campaigns targeting think tanks focused on North Korean affairs.

T1059.007.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript
T1059.007.simple: Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1059.007.detailed: Embedded strings: `WScript.Shell`, `eval(`, `ActiveXObject`, `.js` references; Node.js binary artifacts
T1059.007.real_world_example: FIN7 used obfuscated JavaScript files to deliver their Carbanak backdoor in 2017-2018 campaigns against US restaurant and hospitality companies.

T1559.001.name: Inter-Process Communication: Component Object Model
T1559.001.simple: Inter-Process Communication: Component Object Model — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1559.001.detailed: Imports `CoCreateInstance`, `CoInitialize`, `CoInitializeEx`, `CLSIDFromProgID`, `OleInitialize`; embedded CLSIDs in binary data
T1559.001.real_world_example: MuddyWater exploited DDE in Word documents to execute PowerShell targeting Middle Eastern governments (2017-2018).

T1559.002.name: Inter-Process Communication: Dynamic Data Exchange
T1559.002.simple: Inter-Process Communication: Dynamic Data Exchange — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1559.002.real_world_example: APT32 used COM object hijacking for inter-process code execution in Southeast Asian espionage (2017-2019).

T1204.002.name: User Execution: Malicious File
T1204.002.simple: User Execution: Malicious File — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1204.002.detailed: Document with auto-execute macros (`AutoOpen`, `AutoExec`, `Document_Open`); PE disguised with document icon in resources
T1204.002.real_world_example: Emotet distributed malicious Word documents with macros via email spam from 2018-2020, becoming one of the most prolific malware distribution operations globally.

T1547.002.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Authentication Package
T1547.002.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Authentication Package — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.002.real_world_example: Carbanak group registered malicious DLLs as Windows authentication packages under LSA (2015).

T1547.004.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Winlogon Helper DLL
T1547.004.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Winlogon Helper DLL — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.004.real_world_example: Winnti Group used Winlogon Helper DLL modifications to load backdoors during login (2020).

T1547.005.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Security Support Provider
T1547.005.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Security Support Provider — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.005.real_world_example: Turla registered a malicious DLL as an SSP under LSA Security Packages, loading into lsass.exe (2016).

T1547.006.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Kernel Modules and Extensions
T1547.006.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Kernel Modules and Extensions — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.006.detailed: ELF with `.ko` extension or kernel module magic; embedded strings: `insmod`, `modprobe`, `/lib/modules/`
T1547.006.real_world_example: APT28 loaded malicious kernel modules on compromised Linux systems for persistent access (2016).

T1547.010.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Port Monitors
T1547.010.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Port Monitors — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.010.real_world_example: APT37 modified the Port Monitors registry key to load a DLL with SYSTEM privileges via Print Spooler (2017).

T1547.012.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Print Processors
T1547.012.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Print Processors — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.012.real_world_example: Turla (Snake/Uroburos) registered a malicious DLL as a print processor for SYSTEM-level persistence (2014).

T1547.014.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Active Setup
T1547.014.simple: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Active Setup — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1547.014.real_world_example: APT38 used Active Setup registry keys to execute payloads once per user logon (2018).

T1543.001.name: Create or Modify System Process: Launch Agent (macOS)
T1543.001.simple: Create or Modify System Process: Launch Agent (macOS) — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1543.001.real_world_example: Bundlore adware created Launch Agents in ~/Library/LaunchAgents for persistence across reboots on macOS (2020).

T1543.002.name: Create or Modify System Process: Systemd Service
T1543.002.simple: Create or Modify System Process: Systemd Service — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1543.002.real_world_example: APT32 created Windows services with legitimate-appearing names during campaigns against Southeast Asian organizations (2017).

T1546.002.name: Event Triggered Execution: Screensaver
T1546.002.simple: Event Triggered Execution: Screensaver — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1546.002.detailed: Embedded strings: `SCRNSAVE.EXE`, `\\Control Panel\\Desktop`, `.scr` extension in metadata
T1546.002.real_world_example: Ramnit trojan set SCRNSAVE.EXE to point to malicious executables, executing when screensaver activated (2015).

T1546.003.name: Event Triggered Execution: Windows Management Instrumentation Event Subscription
T1546.003.simple: Event Triggered Execution: Windows Management Instrumentation Event Subscription — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1546.003.real_world_example: Kimsuky used WMI Event Subscriptions for persistence with PowerShell payloads on system startup (2019).

T1546.004.name: Event Triggered Execution: Unix Shell Configuration Modification
T1546.004.simple: Event Triggered Execution: Unix Shell Configuration Modification — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1546.004.real_world_example: APT33 modified .bash_profile and .bashrc on compromised Linux systems to run scripts on terminal open (2018).

T1546.008.name: Event Triggered Execution: Accessibility Features
T1546.008.simple: Event Triggered Execution: Accessibility Features — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
T1546.008.detailed: Embedded strings: `sethc.exe`, `utilman.exe`, `osk.exe`, `narrator.exe`, `magnify.exe`; import of Image File Execution Options registry path
T1546.008.real_world_example: APT3 replaced sethc.exe (Sticky Keys) with cmd.exe for SYSTEM access at the login screen (FireEye 2014).

T1546.009.name: Event Triggered Execution: AppCert DLLs
T1546.009.simple: Event Triggered Execution: AppCert DLLs — a technique attackers use that Anya can detect by examining the file's structure, imports, or embedded strings.
